name: Bourgeois Rounded
classification_notes: A rounded variant of the geometric sans Bourgeois, with clean contemporary letterforms echoing late 20th century modernism. The rounded terminals are characteristic of the rounded geometric sans subcategory.
summary: A rounded geometric sans built on the Bourgeois framework, offering 24 styles across regular and condensed widths for short, expressive text.
description: Grown from the Bourgeois geometric family, this rounded variant softens the original's pared-down asceticism into something more dynamic and ephemeral. The family spans 24 styles across Bourgeois Rounded and Bourgeois Rounded Condensed, making it versatile for display and short text settings. Its rounded terminals sit in a tradition that is simultaneously ultramodern and nostalgic, lending itself to work requiring urgency or a degree of playfulness.
foundry_blurb: Barnbrook describe Bourgeois Rounded as built upon the Bourgeois geometric framework, contemporary in look and feel and echoing late 20th century modernism. They note the complex history of rounded typefaces, valued for both legibility and fashionable qualities, and position the family's 24 styles as particularly suited to short text requiring urgency or playfulness.

name: Bourgeois
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a squarish geometric font drawing on mid-century modernism and the lineage of Renner and Novarese; the squarish geometry places it firmly in the geometric sans subcategory.
summary: A squarish geometric sans with 24 styles, drawing on the mid-century modernist tradition of Renner and Novarese while incorporating stylistic alternates rooted in Italian Futurist letterforms.
description: Built on the squarish geometry of mid-century modernism, Bourgeois takes cues from Paul Renner and Aldo Novarese while adding a contemporary warmth that softens its technological sheen. The family spans 24 styles, making it one of Barnbrook's most extensive releases, and includes a set of stylistic alternates derived from early Italian Futurist letterforms for a more radical register. Best suited to branding and identity work where geometric precision needs a degree of character.
foundry_blurb: Barnbrook describe Bourgeois as a squarish geometric font that reinterprets mid-century modernism with a contemporary flavour, referencing the visual language of Aldo Novarese and Paul Renner. It includes stylistic alternates based on early Italian Futurist letterforms, and its name references both the traditional typographic term for 9pt letterpress type and the social class that was the target of upheaval when geometric type was first being developed.

name: Bastard
classification_notes: Explicitly a blackletter typeface, digitally assembled using a modular system that acknowledges historical blackletter rhythm and drama while transforming it through computer-aided production methods.
summary: A modular blackletter drawn on a personal computer in 1988, referencing sources from the Gutenberg Bible to Dürer while embedding deliberate political and consumerist commentary into individual letterforms.
description: Originally drawn in 1988 using primitive font design software and published around 1990, Bastard applies William Morris's dictum that the tool should be acknowledged in the form of the design. Its letterforms were assembled digitally using a modular system, producing a blackletter that sits between historical tradition and contemporary production methods. Specific glyphs carry pointed references: the uppercase R alludes to the fascist boot, the uppercase Y to the Yen symbol, and the lowercase of the Spindly weight echoes a barcode.
foundry_blurb: Barnbrook describe Bastard as a contemporary blackletter and one of the first typefaces created using a personal computer, drawn in 1988 with primitive font design software and published two years later. They cite William Morris's idea that the tool should be acknowledged in the form of the design as a direct influence, noting that Bastard's modular digital assembly transforms historical blackletter rhythm and drama into something aligned with contemporary modes of production. Sources range from the Gutenberg Bible to Albrecht Dürer's geometric experiments, with individual letterforms referencing fascist and consumerist concepts.

name: Coma
classification_notes: Coma's modular, square construction and vertical/horizontal setting capability place it firmly in the geometric display category, designed for striking typographic configurations rather than extended text use.
summary: A modular, square-profile display typeface designed to sit alongside Japanese typography, set horizontally or vertically.
description: Built on a strict square grid, Coma is intended as a companion to Japanese type, where its proportions align naturally with the fixed-width character blocks of CJK setting. The modular construction gives it flexibility across orientations, making it suited to packaging, editorial and identity work where Latin and Japanese type must coexist. Its name references a state of overwhelm in response to modern information culture.
foundry_blurb: Barnbrook describe Coma as originally designed for use alongside Japanese typography, with a square form enabling both horizontal and vertical setting. They note its striking profile, modular construction and contemporary character as allowing use across a wide range of configurations, and link its name to the paralysis and exhaustion of contemporary life under constant news and stimuli.

name: Delux
classification_notes: A display typeface drawing on 1950s pulp sci-fi and painted military lettering, giving it a retro-futuristic character distinct from conventional text or body type categories.
summary: A display typeface channelling 1950s pulp science fiction and painted military lettering, built around a retro-futuristic tension between optimism and longing.
description: Delux draws on the graphic language of mid-century pulp sci-fi novels and military painted letterforms to produce something that feels simultaneously forward-looking and nostalgic. The name itself is an ironic provocation, critiquing the designer-label economy and the manufactured desirability that detaches objects from their designed function. It sits naturally in editorial and cultural projects where conceptual weight matters as much as visual style.
foundry_blurb: Barnbrook describe Delux as dynamic and urgent in style, drawing influence from 1950s pulp science fiction novels and painted military letterforms. It evokes retro-futuristic nostalgia, containing both naive optimism for a future yet to come and melancholic longing for a time when belief in that future was near-universal. The name is an ironic comment on designer branding, and the foundry accompany it with a sardonic label positioning it as your life-enhancing, environment-destroying, inequality-producing designer typeface.

name: Bourgeois Slab
classification_notes: Built upon the geometric Bourgeois sans framework, Bourgeois Slab applies slab serifs to an otherwise contemporary geometric structure, echoing mid-century modernism rather than Victorian or transitional precedents.
summary: A geometric slab serif drawn from the same framework as Barnbrook's Bourgeois, adding sturdy serifs and a warmer, more expressive character suited to branding and text-heavy work.
description: Where Bourgeois is pared-down and ascetic, Bourgeois Slab introduces slab serifs that bring friendliness and authority without abandoning the geometric underpinning. Combined with Bourgeois Slab Condensed, the family spans 24 styles covering a wide range of text sizes and layouts. It is particularly directed at branding, logo work and editorial settings that require a genial but authoritative tone.
foundry_blurb: Barnbrook describe Bourgeois Slab as built upon the framework of Bourgeois, their popular geometric type family, with letterforms that are thoroughly contemporary yet echo mid-century modernism. They note that slab serifs carry connotations of sturdiness, durability and trustworthiness rooted in their Industrial Revolution origins, and that Bourgeois Slab and Slab Condensed together offer 24 styles suited to text-heavy projects and branding work requiring an authoritative but genial voice.

name: Doctrine Sans
classification_notes: The typeface is explicitly described as drawing from classic mid-century neo-grotesques, with a large x-height, low contrast and squarer heavier weights, while incorporating humanist and geometric alternate characters.
summary: A neo-grotesque sans in five weights with true italics, drawing on mid-century models while incorporating humanist and geometric alternate characters.
description: Developed from the unexpected starting point of North Korean airline livery, Doctrine Sans brings a contemporary warmth to the neo-grotesque tradition. Lighter weights carry a rounded, composed character, while heavier cuts rely on a large x-height, low contrast and squarer forms to deliver something more assertive. True italics across all five weights, a range of ligatures and multiple figure styles mark it out from the standard oblique-only neo-grotesque template.
foundry_blurb: Barnbrook describe Doctrine Sans as a contemporary sans-serif with an agreeable character, positioned as the moderate companion to Doctrine Stencil. Beginning from the North Korean national airline livery as an obscure reference point, the typeface was developed through mature typographic influences including classic mid-century neo-grotesques. Alternate characters borrow from humanist and geometric styles, while true italics, ligatures and varied figure styles give it flexibility as a text face.

name: Doublethink
classification_notes: Derived from hand-lettered shop signage from 1960s Yugoslavia, the design has an expressive, manually constructed quality with a bold inline variant that places it firmly in the display category.
summary: A two-weight display typeface revived from 1960s Yugoslav shop-front lettering, with a bold inline cut that carries the weight of its political origins.
description: Developed from lettering drawn by Vinko Ožić-Pajić for Standard Konfekcija, the first fashion brand in Communist Yugoslavia, the design translates hand-drawn signage into a digital typeface offered in Medium and Bold Inline. The source material carries genuine historical specificity: a military fabric company turned fashion retailer, known for introducing plastic bags to the country, now vanished after the fall of Communism. Barnbrook named it after Orwell's concept from Nineteen Eighty-Four, pointing at the contradictions embedded in its origins and their resonance in contemporary surveillance culture.
foundry_blurb: Barnbrook describe Doublethink as derived from 1960s lettering by Vinko Ožić-Pajić for Yugoslavian state-owned clothes company Standard Konfekcija, the first fashion brand in Communist Yugoslavia. The typeface is offered in two weights — Medium and Bold Inline — and takes its name from Orwell's Nineteen Eighty-Four, referencing the act of holding two contradictory beliefs simultaneously, a concept Barnbrook consider increasingly relevant in the present era of surveillance.

name: Doctrine Stencil
classification_notes: Classified as display due to its stencil construction and headline-focused design. Blends neo-grotesque, humanist and geometric influences with a stencil treatment, making it unsuitable for continuous text.
summary: A stencil display face derived from North Korean airline livery, blending neo-grotesque, humanist and geometric influences with a playful approach to the stencil form.
description: Developed from the livery of North Korea's national airline, Doctrine Stencil sits alongside the text typeface Doctrine as its more expressive, headline-oriented counterpart. It draws on neo-grotesque, humanist and geometric letterform traditions while adapting them to a stencil model, balancing muscular presence with contemporary refinement. Four alternate character sets, a range of ligatures and multiple figure styles give it considerable typographic flexibility for a display face.
foundry_blurb: Barnbrook describe Doctrine Stencil as a display typeface with a muscular character and the revolutionary comrade of their text face Doctrine. Developed from the North Korean national airline livery, it retains the idiosyncratic spirit of that source while evolving into a more contemporary headline face, drawing on neo-grotesque, humanist and geometric styles with a playful approach to the stencil model.

name: Echelon
classification_notes: Based on 1970s Eastern European 'pipe-style' lettering with a deliberately stylised, fashion-inflected aesthetic rooted in Communist consumer typography; clearly a display face rather than text.
summary: A revival of 1970s Eastern European pipe-style lettering, named after the classified international surveillance network.
description: Drawn from the peculiar strand of Communist consumer typography that flourished in Eastern Europe during the 1970s, Echelon has letterforms that share historical roots with Western type yet carry their own distinctly strange fashionable-unfashionable character. The name references the classified government surveillance network said to monitor satellite communications, bank accounts and private correspondence worldwide. Barnbrook frames the parallel between Soviet-era surveillance and contemporary Western intelligence practices as central to the typeface's identity.
foundry_blurb: Barnbrook describe Echelon as derived from 1970s Eastern European pipe-style typefaces, a form of Communist consumer typography that felt, at the time, like it came from another universe. The naming references Echelon, the codename for a covert international surveillance system capable of monitoring communications and tracking individuals, which officially does not exist.

name: Exocet
classification_notes: Based on primitive Greek and Roman stone carving, Exocet is a display typeface drawing on ancient inscriptional letterforms rather than Renaissance revival conventions, placing it firmly in the display/archaic category.
summary: A display typeface reinterpreting primitive Greek and Roman stone-carved letterforms as a contemporary visual language, not as revival.
description: Drawing on ancient Greek and Roman inscription rather than Renaissance typefaces, Exocet deliberately sidesteps pastiche to arrive at something with genuine archaic weight and idiosyncrasy. It has found wide use across food packaging, video games and cultural projects, pointing to the versatility of its rough-edged authority. The family now covers Cyrillic and Central European languages alongside Latin, broadening its reach considerably.
foundry_blurb: Barnbrook describe Exocet as rooted in primitive Greek and Roman stone carving, a source they regard as uniquely beautiful and underappreciated compared with the Renaissance work that dominates typographic inspiration. Rather than a revival or pastiche, the ancient letterforms were consciously reinterpreted as a visual language for today. The name itself references the French anti-ship missile and the danger inherent in language, the capacity of words to start wars or make peace, to soothe or vilify.

name: Drone
classification_notes: Drone draws on hand-drawn ecclesiastical lettering and 16th-century English calligraphic forms, placing it firmly in the display category with a calligraphic subcategory. Its deliberately misproportioned, naive character distinguishes it from conventional script or serif families.
summary: A deliberately misproportioned display face drawn from naive hand-lettered Catholic church signage found in the Philippines and Los Angeles.
description: Inspired by amateur lettering in Spanish/Hispanic Catholic churches that attempted to echo the grandeur of the Vatican but arrived somewhere entirely their own, Drone captures the charm of forms that are copies of copies. The letterforms also carry traces of 16th-century English calligraphy, giving the face an unexpected historical depth beneath its vernacular surface. Barnbrook frames it conceptually as the low hum of advertising and programmed persuasion, lending the typeface a pointed cultural edge.
foundry_blurb: Barnbrook describe Drone as a deliberately misproportioned typeface inspired by hand-drawn lettering in Spanish/Hispanic Catholic churches in the Philippines and Los Angeles. They note these naive letterforms were attempts to recreate Vatican grandeur that instead produced something unique, and also bear resemblance to 16th-century English calligraphy. Conceptually, they position Drone as the sound of advertising that targets aspirations, insecurities and fears.

name: Hopeless Diamond
classification_notes: A display typeface with angular, faceted letterforms derived from stealth aircraft geometry and 19th century carved lettering, placing it firmly in the display category with a geometric and dimensional character.
summary: Three-style display family drawn from the faceted geometry of stealth aircraft and 19th century carved lettering, with italics and alternate character sets throughout.
description: The letterforms take their dimensional, angular structure from the B-2 Spirit and F-117 Nighthawk stealth aircraft, reinterpreted through the tradition of 19th century carved type. Three styles each include an italic and an alternate character set, offering a wide range of combinable permutations. The name comes from the sardonic nickname test pilots gave an early F-117 prototype, whose radical departure from aerodynamic convention they called the Hopeless Diamond.
foundry_blurb: Barnbrook describe Hopeless Diamond as a contemporary display typeface inspired by the dimensionality of 19th century carved lettering and the radical geometry of the B-2 Spirit Stealth Bomber and F-117 Nighthawk. The family comprises three styles, each with an italic and alternate character set for generating varied permutations. The name derives from the derisory term test pilots used for an early F-117 model, which they called the Hopeless Diamond due to its unconventional structure and departure from aerodynamic doctrine.

name: Expletive Script
classification_notes: Despite the 'Script' in its name, Expletive Script is a modular display face built on circular geometry rather than a calligraphic model; its stacking and pattern-making capabilities place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A modular display face built on circular geometry, with characters that shift above and below the baseline to produce ornamental patterns and expressive headlines.
description: Characters can be positioned above or below the baseline, making it as useful for generating repeating decorative patterns as for headline-setting. The circular module gives it a simple, consistent geometry that scales from fine detail to large-format display work. Its name references the social power of profanity, framing typography as the visual embodiment of language's more transgressive registers.
foundry_blurb: Barnbrook describe Expletive Script as a modular font based on a circular form, whose characters can sit above or below the baseline to create unusual display typography and complex repeating patterns. The name references the alluring power of swear words, acknowledging that profanity carries a unique expressive force and serves as an indicator of social structure and shifting taboos.

name: False Idol
classification_notes: Derived from degraded rub-down lettering rather than formal type design tradition, giving it a raw, vernacular display character rooted in 1970s ephemera.
summary: A display face drawn from degraded 1970s rub-down lettering, pulling its character from pornographic magazines and home-made religious pamphlets in equal measure.
description: False Idol lifts its letterforms from the smudged, imperfect transfer lettering found in 1970s pornographic magazines and amateur religious leaflets, materials that shared the same aspirations to glamour and fell equally short. The result sits in an uncomfortable space between seedy and beautiful, a quality Barnbrook attribute to the naivety of the original sources. That awkward tension has made it one of the foundry's most enduring releases.
foundry_blurb: Barnbrook describe False Idol as rooted in bad rub-down lettering from 1970s pornographic magazines and home-made religious leaflets, noting that the letterforms were meant to suggest glamour but ended up looking seedy. They credit this naive tension between seediness and glamour as the source of the peculiar beauty that has made it one of their most popular fonts.

name: Mason Sans
classification_notes: Explicitly described as a humanist sans-serif, informed by Edward Johnston and Eric Gill, with architectural and early European modernist reference points.
summary: A humanist sans drawn from the serif Mason, shaped by British typographic tradition and early 20th-century London architecture.
description: Mason Sans translates the characters of Mason Serif into a humanist sans-serif with an unusual, considered elegance. Barnbrook cites early European modernism and London's early 20th-century buildings as architectural reference points, alongside the typographic legacy of Edward Johnston and Eric Gill. The result is a sans with a distinctly British sensibility and a formality that sets it apart from more neutral humanist models.
foundry_blurb: Barnbrook describe Mason Sans as the sans-serif counterpart to Mason Serif, with Mason's characters transformed into an elegant and unusual humanist sans. Architectural inspiration comes from early European modernism and early 20th-century London buildings, while typographically the face is informed by the work of Edward Johnston and Eric Gill, both significant influences on Jonathan Barnbrook during his college years.

name: Infidel
classification_notes: Based directly on letterforms from the Lindisfarne Gospels and comparable medieval manuscripts, placing it firmly in the insular/blackletter tradition of early medieval Latin scripts rather than later Gothic textura.
summary: A typeface drawn from the letterforms of the Lindisfarne Gospels, designed in direct response to post-9/11 religious intolerance.
description: Derived from insular manuscript hands of the early medieval period, the letterforms sit at a deliberate distance from contemporary legibility conventions. The confrontational name is set in pointed tension with the serene, devotional source material, making the design as much a political statement as a typographic one. Barnbrook conceived the pairing of historically religious forms with an inflammatory label to expose the contradictions embedded in religious intolerance.
foundry_blurb: Barnbrook describe Infidel as rooted in letters from the Lindisfarne Gospels and kindred medieval bibles, forms they characterise as wonderfully idiosyncratic, some beautiful and some ugly, far removed from what we recognise as legible today. The typeface was made as a reaction to the surge of negative energy directed at religion after September 11th, with the choice of a confrontational name intended to illuminate the inherent contradictions of religious intolerance.

name: Mason Serif
classification_notes: Mason Serif draws on eclectic historical and architectural references including Gothic, Classical and Christian forms, placing it firmly in the display serif category with a decorative, expressive character.
summary: A display serif drawing on European architectural forms, Gothic church windows and classical temples, with a deliberately confrontational naming history.
description: Originally released under the name Manson, a reference to serial killer Charles Manson chosen to juxtapose beauty and violence, the typeface was renamed Mason by distributor Emigre following complaints. Its letterforms pull from the gardens of Versailles, Grecian temple porticos and Gothic tracery, colliding historical references into a single expressive display face. It has since been adopted by major clients including the BBC and Walt Disney.
foundry_blurb: Barnbrook describe Mason as an exploration of European architectural forms, with influences drawn from Versailles, Grecian temple porticos, Gothic church windows and the Christian cross. They note its controversial naming history: originally called Manson after the serial killer Charles Manson, the name was chosen to express extreme opposite emotions — beauty and ugliness, love and hate — before being changed by distributor Emigre following complaints. The typeface has become one of Barnbrook's most widely recognised, used by companies including the BBC and Walt Disney.

name: Moron
classification_notes: Moron is a display typeface rooted in Victorian Tuscan and Grotesque letterforms, blending sans-serif industrial weight with ornamental qualities characteristic of pre-modernist typography. The foundry describes it as a 'cute/nasty, old/new' drawing that defies straightforward grotesque classification.
summary: A display typeface pulling from Victorian Tuscan and Grotesque lettering found in Glasgow's churches and cemeteries, fused with 1970s sausage-font sensibility.
description: Rooted in pre-modernist letterforms from Glasgow's graveyards and ecclesiastical architecture, Moron began as a sans-serif redrawing of Barnbrook's Nylon before evolving into its own distinct character. The design balances the industrial weight of Victorian grotesques with decorative ornamental qualities that predate the clean rationalism of modernism. The result sits somewhere between cute and menacing, old and new, with an idiosyncratic energy that suits display work where generic grotesques fall flat.
foundry_blurb: Barnbrook describe Moron as a distinctively idiosyncratic display typeface combining cute and nasty, old and new qualities, drawing on Victorian Tuscan and Grotesque letterforms found in Glasgow's churches and cemeteries. They note it began as a sans-serif reworking of their typeface Nylon before developing into something entirely its own, and reference the Victorians' capacity to balance industrial functionality with romantic ornamentation in an age of early mass production.

name: Newspeak
classification_notes: A display typeface drawing on Stalinist architectural forms and Cyrillic letterform references, making it firmly a display face with strong vernacular and political character rather than a text or body type category.
summary: A display typeface built from the architectural vocabulary of Stalinist Russia, carrying a deliberate political charge rooted in Orwellian ideas about language and power.
description: Drawing on the monumental forms of Soviet architecture and the visual logic of Cyrillic letterforms, this typeface weaponises a strange, decadent beauty to evoke totalitarian control. Named after the fictional language of George Orwell's Nineteen Eighty-Four, it frames typography as a vehicle for political ideology, where the shape of letters can constrain or manipulate thought. Its release was accompanied by a poster featuring Tony Blair, connecting the typeface's themes to New Labour's media management and the rhetorical sleight of hand that presents neoliberalism as social democracy.
foundry_blurb: Barnbrook describe Newspeak as a display typeface based on the architectural forms of Stalinist Russia, presented with a decadent visual language and a sinister political undertone. They note that Stalinist architecture contains a strange beauty hinting at an unrealised utopia, and that this beauty was historically deployed to enforce brutal dictatorship. The name references Orwell's Nineteen Eighty-Four, acknowledging how typography, as a representation of language, can communicate subtle nuances of thought and political control.

name: Melancholia
classification_notes: A sans-serif with calligraphic roots and old-style italic influences, placing it firmly in the humanist sans tradition where stroke contrast and hand-drawn qualities inform the letterforms.
summary: A calligraphically informed sans-serif with true italics drawn from old-style serif models and swash alternates that introduce emotional warmth into a typically austere classification.
description: Where most sans-serifs lean on geometric or functional principles, Melancholia draws on calligraphic letterforms to bring a quality of wistfulness to the design. Its true italics take their cue from old-style serif italics in the tradition of Claude Garamond, rather than simply slanting the roman. Stylistic alternates and calligraphic swash characters extend the expressive range further, making it well suited to editorial and literary work where tone matters as much as legibility.
foundry_blurb: Barnbrook describe Melancholia as a subtle and beautiful sans-serif inspired by calligraphic letterforms, with a name that evokes deep sadness and an intense sensitivity to the world. The design attempts to introduce wistfulness into the sans-serif form, a classification they characterise as typically austere and functional. The typeface includes true italics influenced by old-style serif italics such as those found in Garamond, alongside stylistic alternates and calligraphic swash characters.

name: Patriot
classification_notes: Derived from early Greek and Roman stone carving traditions, Patriot is a display sans without serifs but with the angular, incised quality of lapidary lettering rather than any modernist grotesque lineage.
summary: A sans-serif companion to Exocet, drawing on early Greek and Roman stone carving to produce a striking lapidary display face with pointed political naming.
description: Where Exocet adapted ancient lapidary letterforms into a serifed display face, Patriot strips the serifs whilst hewing more closely to the original stone-carved source material. Unique and alternative character forms give it a distinct identity beyond its sibling typeface. The name, borrowed from a surface-to-air missile system and the jingoism it implies, is a deliberate provocation about the capacity of language to carry both peace and violence.
foundry_blurb: Barnbrook describe Patriot as the sans-serif version of their Emigre-released typeface Exocet, rooted in early Greek and Roman stone carving yet developed with unique forms and alternative characters to stand as an original typeface in its own right. The name references both the Raytheon missile system and a broader jingoistic nationalism, pointing to language's dual potential to create peace or incite violence.

name: NixonScript
classification_notes: Described as a display typeface with serif characteristics, low x-height and an ecclesiastical quality that emerged from a transformation of original sans-serif vernacular lettering. The italic bold variant and expressive form place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A two-weight display typeface drawn from 1960s American vernacular lettering, transformed into a low x-height serif with an ecclesiastical character and named after Richard Nixon.
description: Beginning as a tracing of lettering found on a 1960s camera from a Chicago junk shop, NixonScript diverged considerably during development, shifting from sans-serif origins to a serifed form with a low x-height and a quality described by Barnbrook as ecclesiastical piousness. Rather than a regular and italic pairing, the family offers a regular alongside a bold italic, with the double emphasis giving the latter an energetic, self-congratulatory character. The name references Nixon's resignation and what Barnbrook frames as a parallel abuse of typographic freedom in the design process.
foundry_blurb: Barnbrook describe NixonScript as a display typeface inspired by the typographic freedom of 1950s and 60s American vernacular typography, loosely based on lettering from a 1960s camera found in a Chicago junk shop. The design transformed substantially during development, moving from sans-serif to serif and gaining a low x-height and ecclesiastical atmosphere. The typeface is named after Richard Nixon, framing his abuse of power as an analogy for the bastardisation of American vernacular typography the design underwent.

name: Nylon / Draylon
classification_notes: Nylon draws on pre-16th century mediaeval letterforms with deliberately distorted, almost demonic character; Draylon is a more restrained companion based on 18th century ceramic lettering. Both are display faces with an intentionally crude digital quality.
summary: A paired display family drawing on pre-16th century mediaeval letterforms and 18th century ceramic lettering, both redrawn with deliberate digital crudeness.
description: Nylon takes its shapes from mediaeval portraits at the National Gallery in London, where classical letterforms have warped into something almost demonic over centuries of reproduction. Draylon is its more composed companion, sourced from 18th century ceramic lettering some 200 years later in stylistic lineage, and the two are designed to be mixed together. Both are drawn with an acknowledged digital awkwardness, treating the computer as a visible part of the making process rather than a neutral tool.
foundry_blurb: Barnbrook describe Nylon as an interpretation of pre-16th century letterforms found in mediaeval portraits at the National Gallery, London, noting that the source material contains unusual, manic shapes that appear perverted and almost demonic. Draylon is the more restrained counterpart, based on letterforms from 18th century ceramics, and both are designed to be mixed together. The names reference mid-20th century synthetic materials — Nylon famously coined from New York and London — both of which began as symbols of glamour and came to signify tackiness.

name: Priori Acute
classification_notes: Priori Acute is a display typeface with deliberately distorted, illusionistic letterforms inspired by M.C. Escher; its shifting perspectives and self-folding shapes make it unsuitable for text and firmly in the decorative display category.
summary: A display typeface from the Priori family that uses Escher-inspired optical illusions to create letterforms that shift and distort at larger sizes.
description: Part of Barnbrook's Priori series, which explores shared core character shapes across different stylistic treatments. Priori Acute introduces incongruous perspectives and self-folding shapes drawn from the spatial paradoxes of M.C. Escher's work. At small sizes the letterforms read conventionally, but scaled up for headlines, initial caps or logotypes, the characters begin to confuse and trick the eye in ways that reward closer inspection.
foundry_blurb: Barnbrook describe Priori Acute as a tangential take on the Priori family, drawing heavily on M.C. Escher's work to produce letterforms with incongruous perspectives and twisting shapes that fold into themselves. The effect is subtle at small sizes but grows increasingly disorienting when the type is enlarged, making it best suited to initial caps, short headlines and logos.

name: Priori Sans
classification_notes: Priori Sans draws on early 20th-century London typography, giving it the humanist warmth and historical grounding typical of humanist sans-serifs rather than the neutrality of grotesque or geometric approaches.
summary: A sans-serif text face designed alongside a serif counterpart, both sharing a common core character structure rooted in early 20th-century London typography.
description: Part of the Priori system, which proposes that serif and sans-serif variants share a common underlying character shape so they can be mixed within the same word or setting. Barnbrook drew directly from the early 20th-century typographic culture of London, giving the Sans a grounded, place-specific quality rather than a neutral or abstract one. The paired-family approach makes it suited to editorial and book work where serif and sans need to coexist without visual friction.
foundry_blurb: Barnbrook describe Priori as a proposal for a series of typefaces with different styles but a common core character shape, enabling them to be mixed together in the same word. They note that the design borrows heavily from early 20th-century London typography, reflecting their belief in being true to one's own surroundings and cultural experience.

name: Priori Serif
classification_notes: Priori Serif draws from early 20th-century British typography, particularly Eric Gill and Edward Johnston, giving it a humanist character rooted in calligraphic and inscriptional traditions, with experimental letterform details layered over that classical base.
summary: A text serif rooted in early 20th-century British letterform tradition, designed to mix with its sans-serif counterpart within the same word.
description: Part of the Priori family, which pairs a serif and a sans sharing a common underlying structure so they can be set together without friction. The design draws on Eric Gill and Edward Johnston's work in London, treating their calligraphic and inscriptional models as a starting point before introducing experimental characters that push those traditional forms. Ultra-lightweight cuts are included for signage and large-scale display use.
foundry_blurb: Barnbrook describe Priori as a major text font available in both serif and sans-serif forms, unified by a shared core character structure that allows the two styles to be mixed within the same word. The design borrows heavily from early 20th-century London typography, citing Eric Gill and Edward Johnston as key inspirations, and includes experimental characters that extend those traditional letterforms into a contemporary context, alongside ultra-lightweight versions intended for signage and large-size use.

name: Prototype
classification_notes: Prototype deliberately blurs the boundaries between serif and sans-serif, uppercase and lowercase, making it an experimental display typeface that resists conventional classification.
summary: An experimental typeface from Barnbrook that collapses the distinction between serif and sans-serif, and between uppercase and lowercase, into a single unified alphabet.
description: Prototype emerged from a period of design's own identity crisis, posing the question of whether a rational, universal alphabet is desirable or even coherent. It merges serif and sans-serif letterforms and sidesteps the uppercase/lowercase divide, producing something that is none of those things entirely. The result is a conceptual piece that interrogates the conventions of type design rather than serving conventional typographic needs.
foundry_blurb: Barnbrook describe Prototype as a typeface with a contemporary identity crisis, asking whether it is old or new, uppercase or lowercase, serif or sans-serif. They frame it as part of a long tradition of attempts to create a universal typeface that rationalises the alphabet, carried out at a moment when design itself was experiencing an identity crisis of its own.

name: Prozac
classification_notes: Prozac reduces the Roman alphabet to six geometric shapes, rotated and flipped to produce all 26 letters. This extreme rationalisation places it firmly in experimental display territory, descended from early modernist geometric abstraction.
summary: A conceptual typeface that reduces the Roman alphabet to just six geometric shapes, rotated and flipped to form all 26 letters.
description: Pushing early modernist experiments in alphabetic simplification to an extreme, the typeface constructs every letter from a set of six forms through rotation and reflection alone. Named after the antidepressant, the design deliberately evokes a clinical, pharmaceutical aesthetic, as though produced by scientists rather than type designers. The name also alludes to the numbing effect a hyper-rationalised alphabet might have on language and meaning.
foundry_blurb: Barnbrook describe Prozac as extending the modernist utopian experiment of reducing the alphabet to basic geometric shapes, taking it further by using just six shapes flipped or rotated to produce all 26 letters. The name was chosen for its pharmaceutical aesthetic and to evoke the potential numbing effect of a radically simplified alphabet on language and thought.

name: Regime
classification_notes: Regime is explicitly a slab serif, drawing on the 19th-century Egyptian slab tradition originating with Vincent Figgins, with contemporary detailing and extreme display weights referencing woodblock poster types.
summary: A slab serif with roots in the early 19th-century Egyptian tradition, running from light text weights through to an extreme ultra display cut inspired by woodblock poster types.
description: Drawing on Vincent Figgins's 1815 Antique as its historical touchstone, Regime takes the industrial-era slab serif and redraws it with contemporary detailing and a set of alternate characters. The weight range stretches from Light through to Ultra, the heaviest being a direct nod to the oversize woodblock types of 19th-century poster printing. The name references Britain's rise as an imperial power at the moment the slab serif form first appeared.
foundry_blurb: Barnbrook describe Regime as a slab serif born from the collision of historical influence and contemporary sensibility. They trace the form back to Vincent Figgins's Antique of 1815, positioning it at the moment Britain emerged as a dominant imperial power, and note that the family includes alternate characters and a weight range running up to an extreme ultra based on 19th-century woodblock poster types.

name: Sarcastic
classification_notes: Classified as display due to its monoline construction and decorative intent; it draws from 1950s script typography and neon lettering rather than functioning as a conventional script face.
summary: A monoline display typeface rooted in 1950s phototypesetting scripts and the interlocking rhythms of mid-century neon signage.
description: Sarcastic takes its formal cues from two postwar phenomena: the explosion of script typography enabled by phototypesetting in the early 1950s, and the heyday of elaborate neon signage across American towns and cities. The monoline construction echoes the continuous tube of a neon sign, while the letterforms reference the overlapping, rhythmic quality that phototypesetting made possible. Barnbrook frame the name around an inherent duality, where script's elegant appearance sits in tension with an ironic undertone.
foundry_blurb: Barnbrook describe Sarcastic as a monoline display typeface drawing on the visual language of mid-twentieth century consumerism, specifically 1950s script typography and the interconnected forms of neon lettering. They trace its context to the postwar economic boom, when phototypesetting simplified overlapping characters and neon signage reached its peak, particularly in the United States. The name reflects the duality they see in script typefaces, elegant and easy-reading on the surface, yet ironic in tone.

name: Shock and Awe
classification_notes: A display family derived directly from military stencil lettering found on aircraft and missiles; the industrial, utilitarian source material places it firmly in the display/stencil category.
summary: A two-face display family drawn from stencil lettering on military hardware, one sourced from the B-29 that dropped the atomic bomb on Hiroshima, the other from the Tomahawk cruise missile.
description: Enola Gay takes its letterforms from the nose cone of the B-29 Superfortress bomber used in the 1945 Hiroshima attack, while Tomahawk is derived from fuselage stencilling on the cruise missile deployed in both the 1991 Gulf War and the 2003 Iraq invasion. Barnbrook positions both faces explicitly as a provocation, inviting the design community to examine the ethics of letterform-making in contexts of mass violence. The conceptual weight of the source material is inseparable from how these faces should be used.
foundry_blurb: Barnbrook describe Shock and Awe as a family of two display typefaces drawn from lettering at the centre of major historical events. They present the controversial source material as a deliberate starting point for debate within the design community, with the aim of focusing discussion on context, significance and the ethical considerations involved in creating letterforms for military purposes.

name: Resolution
classification_notes: The family spans three stylistic variants — blackletter, slab and sans — all drawn from hand-painted mural lettering, making 'display' the most accurate umbrella category given the family's expressive, politically charged character.
summary: A three-variant display family redrawn from hand-painted political murals across Northern Ireland, spanning loyalist and republican lettering traditions.
description: Barnbrook drew the six weights of Resolution directly from murals found in Belfast and Derry, tracing a visual history that runs from loyalist street painting through to the famous 'You are now entering Free Derry' wall. The blackletter combines lettering from both loyalist and republican sources; the sans derives primarily from loyalist murals; the slab is lifted from that single celebrated Free Derry corner, a site associated with the Battle of the Bogside and Bloody Sunday. The result is a typeface family with a specific political and geographic origin that no generic revival could replicate.
foundry_blurb: Barnbrook describe Resolution as a creative response to the political situation in Northern Ireland, developed alongside photography, murals, artworks and infographics. The typefaces are redrawings of lettering from some of the region's most significant murals, preserving the hand-drawn quality of a tradition stretching back decades. The family spans three stylistic variants — Blackletter, Slab and Sans — across six weights, each rooted in distinct mural sources from Belfast and Derry.

name: Tourette
classification_notes: Drawn from 19th-century French light slab serifs, with hairline serifs and delicate stroke contrast placing it firmly in the display slab category.
summary: A two-variant display slab rooted in 19th-century French light slab serifs, offering a plain cut and an ornamented one.
description: Based on delicate French slab serif forms from the 19th century, Tourette comes in two versions: Normal, which is restrained and legible, and Extreme, which layers on flourishes for a more decorative character. The name references Tourette's syndrome and the Barnbrook preoccupation with language and obscenity, providing a pointed contrast to the letterforms' lightness. The Extreme cut in particular suits display and titling work where a period decorative sensibility is wanted.
foundry_blurb: Barnbrook describe Tourette as drawing inspiration from 19th-century French light slab serifs, an area they consider particularly beautiful but underexplored in contemporary design. The typeface comes in two versions: Normal, which is simple and legible, and Extreme, which adds flourishes for an exquisite but slightly frivolous feel. The name references Tourette's syndrome and connects to Barnbrook's ongoing interest in the power of language and the magic of swear words, offering a deliberate contrast between the delicacy of the letterforms and the connotations of the name.

name: Virus DejaVu
classification_notes: Derived from Bitstream Vera and DejaVu, the family spans sans-serif, serif and monospace variants; the primary category is sans-serif following the DejaVu lineage, with humanist proportions inherited from Vera.
summary: A manually respaced and partially redrawn adaptation of the open-source DejaVu fonts, developed by Barnbrook for the Mystetskyi Arsenal cultural centre and the Arsenale 2012 biennale in Kyiv.
description: Built atop the open-source DejaVu project, itself a development of Bitstream Vera originally designed by Jim Lyles in 2002, this family covers 13 weights across sans-serif, serif and monospace styles. Barnbrook manually respaced the originals and redrew select characters to suit the visual identity requirements of two major Ukrainian cultural commissions. Released under the same open-source licence as its predecessors, permitting derivative works and free redistribution.
foundry_blurb: Barnbrook describe Virus DejaVu as a family built from the collaborative open-source DejaVu project, adapted for the visual identities of Mystetskyi Arsenal and Arsenale 2012 in Kyiv. Choosing an existing open-source font as the typographic foundation was, in their words, a deliberate acknowledgement of digital technology and online creative communities, prioritising the idea of collaboration over refined aesthetics. The family is released under the same open-source licence as the originals.

name: State Machine
classification_notes: A display typeface drawing on stencilled military lettering from Cold War-era vehicles and hand-made political banners, placing it firmly in the display category with a utilitarian, propaganda-inflected character.
summary: A display typeface drawn from Cold War military vehicle lettering and 1970s hand-painted political banners, with a name that bridges political theory and computer science.
description: The letterforms take their cues from stencilled signage on American and Russian Cold War military hardware, with an alternate character set referencing the rough, hand-made quality of 1970s protest banners. The name draws on two distinct definitions: Max Weber's conception of the state as the entity holding a monopoly on legitimate physical force, and the computer science model of an abstract machine cycling through finite states. Together they frame the typeface as a tool for transforming meaning, sitting at the intersection of political language and systematic design.
foundry_blurb: Barnbrook describe State Machine as a display typeface inspired by lettering on American and Russian Cold War military vehicles, with an alternate character set taken from 1970s hand-made political banners. The name is drawn from both political theory, where the state machine describes bureaucratic and repressive state apparatuses, and computer programming, where it describes an abstract computational model that shifts between finite states in response to triggering conditions. Combined, they suggest a tool capable of transforming society.

name: Olympukes 2012
classification_notes: A set of pictographic symbols rather than a conventional typeface; classified as display due to its purely symbolic, non-alphabetic character set used for editorial and satirical purposes.
summary: A satirical pictogram font released for the 2012 London Olympics, extending Barnbrook's ongoing Olympukes project to critique the contradictions and commercialism of the modern Games.
description: Originally begun in 2004, the Olympukes project uses pictographic symbols in the style of official Olympic signage to tell an unofficial, critical story of the Games. The 2012 edition responds to the specific context of London hosting: austerity politics, surveillance culture, and corporate excess. It functions as political commentary delivered through the visual language of sport iconography.
foundry_blurb: Barnbrook describe Olympukes 2012 as a new set of pictograms released for the London Olympics, extending their unofficial project from 2004 to address the geopolitical shifts of the intervening eight years. The font confronts the contradictions of the modern Olympics, including economic crisis in Greece, UK austerity, excessive security measures and rampant commercialism, while noting that Barnbrook has no affiliation with or endorsement from the International Olympic Committee.

name: Olympukes
classification_notes: A set of satirical pictograms rather than a conventional typeface; categorised as display due to its purely graphic, non-alphabetic character set intended for visual communication.
summary: A set of satirical pictograms skewering the corruption and contradictions of the modern Olympic Games, conceived as a counterpoint to the anodyne official iconography.
description: Born from Barnbrook's frustration with the sanitised symbolism of Olympic pictogram design, Olympukes reframes the sporting icons to acknowledge the greed, manipulation and skulduggery that characterise the modern Games. The name fuses 'olympics' and 'puke' as a blunt statement of intent. It sits firmly in the tradition of politically engaged graphic design, using the designer's own toolkit against the spectacle it normally serves.
foundry_blurb: Barnbrook describe Olympukes as emerging from dissatisfaction with past Olympic pictograms, which they felt portrayed an unrealistic ideal of human endeavour whilst ignoring the corruption endemic to the Games. The family offers an alternative set of symbols that wittily and accurately reflect the complex contradictions of the modern Olympics. Barnbrook note they have no affiliation with or endorsement from the International Olympic Committee or any associated body.

name: Virus VujaDe
classification_notes: A display typeface built from remixed glyphs drawn from DejaVu, combining symbols, foreign scripts and analphabetic characters to produce sequentially alternating letterforms. Its experimental, disorienting character places it firmly in the display category.
summary: An open-source display typeface built for the Kyiv Biennale 2012 that cycles through four alternating glyphs per character, drawn from the DejaVu font's extended glyph palette.
description: Constructed by remixing the DejaVu open-source font, each Latin and Cyrillic letter or numeral is given four alternating glyph variants drawn from analphabetic characters, foreign scripts and symbols. As the user types, the font sequences through these variants automatically, producing unexpected juxtapositions that read as typographic interference. Developed as the typographic voice of Arsenale 2012, the project deliberately prioritised collaborative, open-source thinking over polished aesthetics.
foundry_blurb: Barnbrook describe Virus VujaDe as a typographic remix project that draws from the extended glyph palette of the DejaVu fonts, combining analphabetic glyphs, foreign scripts and symbols to generate four sequentially alternating characters for each letter and number in Latin and Cyrillic. Created as a core element of the visual identity for the Kyiv Biennale Arsenale 2012, it was released under the same open-source licence as the original DejaVu font, allowing derivative works and free redistribution.

name: Apocalypso
classification_notes: Apocalypso is a symbol and pictogram font rather than a text typeface, containing crosses and illustrative glyphs. It fits best under display as a decorative and conceptual dingbat family.
summary: A pictogram and cross font conceived as an art project around millennial anxiety, first published by Barnbrook in 1997.
description: Originally released in 1997 in the run-up to the millennium, Apocalypso is less a workhorse typeface than a conceptual artefact containing crosses and pictograms organised around themes of end-times. The crosses serve as decorative ornaments within typographic layouts, whilst the pictograms illustrate apocalyptic scenarios that Barnbrook describe as prophetically close to the present day. The name merges 'apocalypse' with 'calypso', nodding to the idea of making light of catastrophe.
foundry_blurb: Barnbrook describe Apocalypso as a pictogram font for the end of the world, its name a portmanteau of apocalypse and calypso, evoking the idiom of fiddling while Rome burns. Originally published in 1997, the family contains crosses for decorative typographic use and pictograms expressing imminent catastrophe. Barnbrook frame it as both a document of millennial-era thinking and a prophetic vision of what has since come to pass.
